items.question: Who uses cross-entropy in machine learning?
items.answer: ML engineers, data scientists, and NLP researchers use it as the default classification and language modeling loss, implemented in frameworks as CrossEntropyLoss and related log-loss metrics.

items.question: What is cross-entropy H(P, Q)?
items.answer: H(P, Q) = −Σ p(x) log q(x), the expected surprise when outcomes follow P but are scored with distribution Q. When P = Q, it equals Shannon entropy H(P).

items.question: When do you minimize cross-entropy during training?
items.answer: During supervised learning when you have true labels (or soft targets) and a model producing class probabilities — each forward pass computes −log q(y) or the full −Σ p log q, then backprop updates parameters.

items.question: Where does cross-entropy appear in a neural classifier?
items.answer: At the output head: logits to softmax define Q, and the loss compares Q to one-hot (or soft) P from the label. The same pattern appears at each vocabulary position in language models.

items.question: Why is cross-entropy preferred over raw misclassification count for training?
items.answer: It provides smooth, probability-sensitive gradients that reward higher confidence on the correct class and heavily penalize confident errors — and it equals negative log-likelihood under standard Bernoulli/Categorical models.

items.question: How does one-hot classification reduce cross-entropy?
items.answer: With p(y)=1 and p(k≠y)=0, H(P, Q) = −log q(y). You only need the model probability on the true class; softmax over logits supplies q(y).

items.question: Cross-entropy vs KL divergence — what changes in the formula?
items.answer: D_KL(P || Q) = H(P, Q) − H(P). KL subtracts the entropy of P; cross-entropy keeps it. On a fixed training set, H(P) is constant, so minimizing cross-entropy is equivalent to minimizing KL, but the numbers differ by H(P).

items.question: Cross-entropy loss vs mean squared error for classification — why choose CE?
items.answer: MSE on one-hot targets does not correspond to Bernoulli/Categorical likelihood and gives weak gradients when predictions are far from labels. Cross-entropy aligns with MLE, is convex in logits, and is the standard for probabilistic classifiers.
